Quantum Computing Primer: Quantum computers exploit quantum mechanical phenomena — superposition, entanglement, and interference — to process information in fundamentally different ways than classical computers. Quantum bits (qubits) can exist in superpositions of 0 and 1 simultaneously, enabling certain algorithms to explore exponentially large solution spaces in polynomial time. Shor's algorithm for integer factorization and Grover's search algorithm are canonical examples of quantum speedups, though practical quantum advantage for real-world problems remains limited by qubit decoherence and error rates. Current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices are being explored for optimization, simulation, and machine learning applications, though fault-tolerant quantum computing likely requires decades of further engineering progress.
